linkQueue.note: Competition law 10-year review. Possible item on what students should know about HK competition enforcement.

items.headline: Hong Kong Issues a Record HK$12 Billion Digital Bond
items.whatsTheNews: In June 2026, the Hong Kong Mortgage Corporation issued a HK$12 billion digital bond, its first public digital bond and one of the largest of its kind anywhere. A digital, or "tokenised," bond is recorded using blockchain technology rather than through traditional paper and ledger systems. Hong Kong has led in this area, having issued the world's first tokenised government green bond back in 2023. The government is pushing more of these deals to build up the market and stay ahead as a finance hub.
items.whatItMeans: A bond is basically a loan: an investor lends money to the issuer, gets paid interest, then gets the money back later. A digital or tokenised bond records who owns it on a blockchain, a shared digital ledger, which can make trading and settlement faster and cheaper. Settlement is the behind-the-scenes process of swapping the cash for the bond, and doing it on a blockchain can cut the time from days to almost instant. Hong Kong wants to lead this technology so it stays competitive as an international financial centre.
clients: Issuers want in on the technology. Companies and public bodies considering digital bonds need advisers who understand both bond law and the technology, which opens new instructions.
Investors need comfort on the rules. Buyers want to know their rights are protected when ownership sits on a blockchain, which raises legal questions to answer.
Market infrastructure firms get involved. Banks and tech providers building these systems need contracts and regulatory advice to run them safely.
lawFirms: It is capital markets work with a technology twist. Firms that combine debt-issuance know-how with digital-asset skills are best placed to win these deals.
It rewards early movers. As Hong Kong pushes tokenisation, firms that act on the first deals build a reputation in a market set to grow.
It creates fresh, modern work for juniors. Digital bonds sit at the cutting edge of finance, giving trainees a skill set few lawyers yet have.

sources.title: HKMA: HKMC's HK$12 billion inaugural public digital bond issuance (11 Jun 2026)

items.headline: Hong Kong Builds a Rulebook for Digital Bonds With a New Expert Group
items.whatsTheNews: On 5 June 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority set up a Tokenised Bond Expert Group to help grow the market for digital bonds. The group brings together banks, lawyers, and market players to work out common standards and tackle the legal and technical hurdles. The aim is to turn one-off digital bond deals into a regular, scalable market. It sits alongside the HKMA's wider tokenisation work, including its Project Ensemble pilot running through 2026.
items.whatItMeans: Tokenisation means turning a financial asset, like a bond, into a digital record on a blockchain. An expert group is a panel the regulator gathers to give advice and agree common ways of doing things, so the market does not grow in a messy, inconsistent way. "Standards" are shared rules and templates that let different banks and systems work together smoothly. The HKMA is trying to lay the groundwork now so Hong Kong becomes a natural home for this kind of finance.
clients: Banks and issuers want a seat at the table. The firms shaping the standards gain an early edge, so they seek advisers who can engage with the regulator's work.
Clear standards lower the risk of acting. Once common rules exist, more companies will feel safe issuing digital bonds, and they will need legal help to do so.
Technology providers need guidance. Firms building the platforms must line up with the new standards, which means legal and regulatory advice.
lawFirms: Lawyers help write the rulebook. Firms involved in the expert group and standard-setting shape the market and become the names clients trust.
It builds a practice for the future. Getting in early on tokenisation positions a firm to win the deals that follow as the market scales.
It blends finance and technology skills. The work suits lawyers who enjoy new areas, and it gives juniors a chance to specialise in something fresh.

sources.title: HKMA: HKMA establishes Tokenised Bond Expert Group (5 Jun 2026)
Timothy Loh: HKMA establishes Tokenised Bond Expert Group

items.headline: Hong Kong Sets Up a New Court for Big Cross-Border Business Disputes
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's Judiciary announced on 28 May 2026 that it will create a Hong Kong International Commercial Court (HKICC) to handle large, complex international business disputes. It will be a specialist division of the High Court, staffed by experienced commercial judges, with the option to invite senior judges from other common-law countries. A dedicated floor of the High Court will lean heavily on technology, including remote hearings, electronic filing, and voice-to-text transcription. The Judiciary aims to have it running within the coming year.
items.whatItMeans: A commercial dispute is a legal fight between businesses, usually over contracts, money, or deals. Cross-border means the parties or the deal involve more than one place, for example a mainland Chinese company and a European one. A specialist division is a dedicated part of the court that handles only one kind of case, so the judges build deep expertise. Judgments from this court can also be recognised and enforced in mainland China under existing arrangements, so winning a case here actually carries weight across the border.
clients: Businesses get a faster, expert venue. Big international disputes are complex, so specialist judges and modern technology mean cases can move more efficiently, which companies value when a lot of money is at stake.

Cross-border enforcement is the real prize. A judgment is only useful if you can actually collect, and because these rulings can be enforced in mainland China, companies have a stronger reason to bring disputes here.

It gives them another option besides arbitration. Many companies currently settle big disputes through private arbitration, so a credible court gives them a fresh route to weigh up, and they will ask lawyers which is better.
lawFirms: It pulls high-value disputes work to Hong Kong. The bigger and more international the cases the city attracts, the more work flows to litigation teams, and these are exactly the high-fee cases firms want.
It plays to cross-border strength. Firms with both Hong Kong and mainland China capability are best placed to act, so this rewards the same firms chasing the China and Hong Kong market.
It signals where the growth is. A new court is a long-term bet on Hong Kong as a disputes hub, so students drawn to litigation or arbitration should watch this closely. The work is set to grow.

sources.title: HK Government: Judiciary announces the HKICC initiative (28 May 2026)
The Standard: HK International Commercial Court to hear cross-border disputes

items.headline: Hong Kong's Economy Grows 5.9%, Its Fastest in Almost Five Years
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's economy grew 5.9% in the first quarter of 2026 compared with a year earlier, its fastest pace in almost five years. The jump was driven by a 23.7% surge in goods exports, helped by strong global demand for AI-related electronics, along with firm spending at home. The government kept its full-year 2026 growth forecast at 2.5% to 3.5%. It flagged that global tensions remain a risk to the outlook.
items.whatItMeans: GDP, or gross domestic product, is the total value of everything a place produces, and its growth rate shows how fast the economy is expanding. Exports are the goods and services a place sells abroad, so a big rise in exports means strong foreign demand, here for the electronics behind AI. A growth rate of 5.9% is high for a developed economy like Hong Kong's, which is why it stands out. A strong economy usually means more business activity, more deals, and more hiring.
clients: A strong economy spurs activity. When growth is healthy, companies are more confident to invest, expand, and do deals, which shapes the advice they need.
Exporters and tech firms ride the wave. Booming demand for electronics helps these clients grow, and growth brings contracts, financing, and expansion plans that need legal support.
Confidence supports fundraising. A healthy economy makes investors keener, so companies find it easier to raise money, often with lawyers guiding the process.
lawFirms: Good times mean more work. A growing economy tends to lift M&A, capital markets, and financing activity, which are the core of a commercial firm's practice.
It supports hiring across teams. When deals and disputes rise together, firms take on more juniors and trainees to cope, which helps applicants.
It is the backdrop to commercial awareness. Understanding why the economy is strong, and what could threaten it, is exactly the kind of insight interviewers test.

sources.title: HK Government: economy grows 5.9% in Q1 (15 May 2026)
SCMP: finance chief says Hong Kong on track for strongest quarterly growth in 5 years

items.headline: Hong Kong's Minimum Wage Rises to HK$43.10 Under a New Yearly System
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's statutory minimum wage rose to HK$43.10 an hour on 1 May 2026, up by HK$1 from HK$42.10. It is the first rise under a new system that reviews the minimum wage every year using a set formula, instead of every two years as before. The government also raised the pay level below which employers must keep detailed records of hours worked. The change applies to all hours worked from 1 May, even part-way through a pay period.
items.whatItMeans: The statutory minimum wage is the lowest hourly pay an employer is legally allowed to give most workers. A review mechanism is the process the government uses to decide whether and how much to raise it, and Hong Kong has switched from a slow two-year cycle to a yearly one driven by a formula. The record-keeping threshold is the pay level below which bosses must log exact hours, which helps prove that low-paid staff are paid at least the minimum. Together these changes make pay rises more regular and easier to enforce.
clients: Employers must update their payroll. Any business paying near the minimum has to raise wages and check its records, so it asks advisers how to comply on time.
Getting it wrong is risky. Underpaying the minimum wage is an offence, so companies want advice to avoid penalties and claims from staff.
Yearly reviews mean yearly planning. Because rises now come every year, businesses with large low-paid workforces need to budget and prepare each time.
lawFirms: Employment teams field a wave of questions. Each wage change brings advisory work on contracts, payroll, and compliance, especially for clients with big workforces.
It can lead to disputes. Pay errors trigger complaints and tribunal claims, which feeds the employment litigation side of the practice.
It keeps employment law in demand. Steady changes like this show why firms keep employment specialists busy, an area where juniors get early client contact.

sources.title: SCMP: Hong Kong's minimum wage set to rise by HK$1 to HK$43.10
Labour Department: Statutory Minimum Wage (official)

items.headline: Hong Kong Holds Interest Rates With the Fed as Rate-Cut Hopes Build
items.whatsTheNews: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority kept the city's base interest rate at 4% in step with the US Federal Reserve, which held its own rate steady. Hong Kong has tied its currency to the US dollar since 1983, so it follows the Fed's moves rather than setting rates on its own. Analysts expect the Fed to cut rates over the course of 2026, which would pull Hong Kong's rates down too. Cheaper borrowing is already feeding through to a recovering property market.
items.whatItMeans: An interest rate is the cost of borrowing money, set as a percentage. Because the Hong Kong dollar is pegged (fixed) to the US dollar, the HKMA has to shadow US rates to keep the peg stable, which is why it moves with the Fed. A base rate is the benchmark that influences what banks charge for loans and mortgages. When rates fall, loans and mortgages get cheaper, which tends to lift spending, business activity, and property prices.
clients: Borrowing costs drive company decisions. When rates look set to fall, businesses are more willing to take loans, buy assets, or do deals, which shapes the advice they seek.
Property and finance clients watch closely. Lower rates lift the property market and make financing cheaper, so developers, lenders, and investors plan their next moves around the rate path.
Timing matters. Companies want to lock in financing or launch deals at the right point in the rate cycle, and they lean on advisers to help them move at the right moment.
lawFirms: Cheaper money means more deals. Falling rates tend to spark more M&A, property transactions, and fundraising, all of which feed corporate, banking, and real estate teams.
Banking and finance teams feel it first. More borrowing and refinancing means more loan documents and security work for the firm.
A busy deal market means more hiring. When transactions pick up, firms need juniors and trainees to handle the volume, which is good news for applicants.

sources.title: SCMP: HKMA mirrors the Fed and keeps the interest rate unchanged
HKMA: response to the US Fed's interest rate decision (30 Apr 2026)

items.headline: HKMA Hands Out Hong Kong's First Stablecoin Licences to HSBC and a Standard Chartered Venture
items.whatsTheNews: On 10 April 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority granted the city's first ever stablecoin issuer licences. They went to HSBC and to Anchorpoint Financial, a joint venture backed by Standard Chartered, the telecoms group HKT, and Animoca Brands. The two were chosen from a large pool of applicants under rules that took effect in 2025. Both plan to launch their Hong Kong dollar stablecoins later in 2026.
items.whatItMeans: A stablecoin is digital money designed to always equal a real currency, here one coin for one Hong Kong dollar. An issuer is the company that creates and stands behind the coin, and it must hold enough safe assets to repay every holder. A licence is official permission to do this, and the HKMA granting only two shows how high the bar is. HSBC has said it wants to plug its stablecoin into everyday tools like its banking app and PayMe.
clients: Winning a licence is just the start. The two issuers now need ongoing legal and compliance advice to launch and run their coins within the rules.
Losing applicants need a new plan. Firms that did not get a licence must decide whether to reapply, partner with a winner, or change their model, and they will seek advice on the options.
Businesses can start using stablecoins. Once these coins launch, companies will want to know the legal and tax treatment of using them for payments, which creates fresh questions for advisers.
lawFirms: A brand-new market means brand-new work. Helping issuers launch, and helping businesses use stablecoins, is advisory work that did not exist before.
Banking, payments, and crypto skills combine. The work rewards firms that can blend financial regulation with technology know-how.
Early movers build the reputation. Firms involved in the first licences become the names clients think of, which pulls in the next wave of digital-money work for juniors to cut their teeth on.

sources.title: HKMA: granting of stablecoin issuer licences (10 Apr 2026)
HSBC: welcomes the HKMA's grant of a stablecoin issuer licence

items.headline: Hong Kong Smashes IPO Records With HK$110 Billion in Three Months
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong raised about HK$109.9 billion from 40 new share listings in the first three months of 2026, its best first quarter in five years. That was roughly six times the amount raised in the same period of 2025. Most of the money came from "A+H" listings, where mainland Chinese companies already listed at home add a Hong Kong listing, which made up 60% of the total. Specialist technology companies listing under a newer set of rules (Chapter 18C) added another HK$19.5 billion.
items.whatItMeans: An IPO is when a company sells its shares to the public for the first time and joins a stock exchange. An A+H listing is when a company already listed in mainland China (the "A" shares) also lists in Hong Kong (the "H" shares) to reach international investors. Chapter 18C is a special part of Hong Kong's rulebook that lets young, fast-growing tech companies list even if they are not yet profitable. A strong IPO quarter means a lot of high-value legal work flowing into the city.
clients: Mainland companies want a Hong Kong listing. It opens access to global investors and a freely usable currency, so more of them are lining up to list and need cross-border legal help.
Tech companies have a route to market. The Chapter 18C rules let promising but loss-making tech firms raise money, so founders and investors seek advice on whether they qualify.
Accuracy is everything in a listing. The paperwork and disclosure are huge, and one error can derail a deal, so companies rely heavily on their advisers.
lawFirms: IPOs are among a firm's biggest earners. Each listing means months of work and large teams, so a record quarter directly boosts revenue.
Cross-border skill wins the biggest deals. Firms with strong Hong Kong and mainland China teams, plus Mandarin-speaking lawyers, land the largest A+H listings.
A full pipeline means more hiring. IPO work needs lots of junior lawyers, so a booming market is one of the clearest signals of trainee demand.

sources.title: SCMP: Hong Kong retains the global IPO crown in the first quarter
KPMG: Hong Kong IPOs raise ~HK$110 billion in Q1 2026

items.headline: Hong Kong's Privacy Watchdog Turns Its Attention to AI and Your Data
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's privacy regulator, the PCPD, has spent 2026 pushing companies to use artificial intelligence without mishandling people's personal data. It has urged organisations to set internal rules on how staff use AI tools and to be clear about when customer data is used to train AI systems. This builds on a model framework it issued for protecting personal data in AI. The watchdog says firms should plan for both innovation and data safety from the start.
items.whatItMeans: The PCPD (Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data) is the body that enforces Hong Kong's data privacy law, the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance. Personal data is information that identifies a living person, like a name, photo, or ID number. Training an AI system often means feeding it large amounts of data, which can include customers' personal data, so the watchdog wants companies to handle that carefully. A framework here is a set of recommended steps a company can follow to stay on the right side of the law while using AI.
clients: Companies using AI need clear rules. Any business adopting AI tools must check how it handles personal data, so it asks advisers to write policies that keep it compliant.
Mishandling data carries real risk. Breaking the privacy law can lead to enforcement, complaints, and reputational damage, so clients want advice before problems arise.
It shapes how products are built. Firms designing AI products need to build in privacy from the start, which means legal input early in the process.
lawFirms: A modern advisory niche is growing fast. Helping clients use AI lawfully blends data privacy, technology, and regulatory work, and demand is rising quickly.
It rewards lawyers who understand technology. Firms that can speak both law and tech win this work, and it is an exciting area for juniors to specialise in.
It feeds future disputes. As AI use spreads, privacy complaints and investigations will follow, giving contentious teams new work down the line.

sources.title: PCPD: aligning with the 15th Five-Year Plan, building privacy safeguards for AI (24 Mar 2026)

items.headline: Hong Kong's 2026-27 Budget Bets on Finance, AI and a First Surplus in Years
items.whatsTheNews: Financial Secretary Paul Chan delivered Hong Kong's 2026-27 Budget on 25 February 2026, reporting an operating surplus earlier than expected after three years of deficits. The theme was driving growth through innovation and finance. He set out steps to strengthen Hong Kong as a financial centre, build up its intellectual property economy, and develop the Northern Metropolis near the mainland border. He also proposed moving HK$150 billion from the Exchange Fund to help pay for infrastructure.
items.whatItMeans: The Budget is the government's yearly plan for how it will raise and spend money. An operating surplus means the government took in more than it spent on day-to-day running, which is a sign the finances are improving. The Exchange Fund is Hong Kong's huge reserve pot, mainly used to defend the currency, so moving money out of it to fund building projects is a notable choice. The Northern Metropolis is a major plan to build homes, offices, and tech parks near the border with mainland China.
clients: Businesses plan around the Budget. Tax changes, fees, and spending priorities affect how companies invest, so they ask advisers what it means for them.

Finance and tech firms see opportunity. Support for the financial sector, intellectual property, and the Northern Metropolis points to where government money and incentives will flow, guiding where clients expand.
Big infrastructure spending creates deals. Funding large projects means contracts, financing, and partnerships, all of which need legal support.
lawFirms: Budget signals shape practice growth. When the government backs finance, tech, and infrastructure, firms build teams around those areas to catch the work.
Tax teams get questions immediately. Companies want to know how any new measures affect them, which is quick, billable advisory work.
Projects work follows the money. Major builds in the Northern Metropolis mean construction, financing, and real estate mandates for years to come.

sources.title: HK Government: 2026-27 Budget Speech by the Financial Secretary (25 Feb 2026)
SCMP: Budget 2026-27 bets big on AI, chips and the Northern Metropolis

items.headline: Hong Kong's Competition Court Lets a HK$150 Million Cartel Penalty Wait
items.whatsTheNews: In a January 2026 ruling, Hong Kong's Court of Appeal looked at how the Competition Tribunal handles penalties and evidence in cartel cases. The case involved ATAL, a building-services contractor that admitted taking part in cartel conduct on air-conditioning and renovation work and agreed to pay HK$150 million in total. The court allowed the Tribunal to hold off setting ATAL's final penalty until related cases against other companies finish, so the penalties stay consistent. It also clarified which documents must be shared between the sides in these cases.
items.whatItMeans: A cartel is a secret deal between competitors to fix prices or carve up work, which harms customers and is illegal. The Competition Tribunal is the special court that hears these cases and decides penalties. Admitting liability means a company accepts it broke the law, often in exchange for a lower penalty. The ruling matters because it shapes the order in which penalties are set and what evidence the sides must hand over, which affects how future cartel cases run.
clients: Companies see how serious enforcement is. A HK$150 million penalty is a loud warning, so businesses want advice to make sure they are not breaking competition law.
Process affects strategy. How and when penalties are set changes whether a company should settle early or fight, so they need lawyers to plan their approach.
Compliance becomes a priority. Firms in industries prone to bid-rigging, like construction, will invest in training and checks to avoid landing in the same spot.
lawFirms: Competition work is growing in Hong Kong. As the watchdog brings more cases, firms get more advisory and defence work, a relatively young field with room to build a name.
These cases are big and complex. They involve large teams, detailed evidence, and high stakes, which means substantial fees and meaty work for juniors.
It spans advice and litigation. Lawyers both help clients avoid breaches and defend them when accused, giving a broad mix of experience.

sources.title: Kennedys: HK Court of Appeal on disclosure and adjourning sentencing in the Competition Tribunal
O'Melveny: Hong Kong Competition Commission brings charges against individuals

items.headline: Hong Kong Moves to License Crypto Dealers, Custodians and Advisers
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong is widening its crypto rulebook in 2026. The Securities and Futures Commission and the government plan to bring in new licences for firms that trade virtual assets, that hold them for clients (custodians), and that advise on or manage them. The aim is to hold crypto firms to the same standards as traditional finance, summed up as "same business, same risks, same rules." New laws are expected to go to the Legislative Council during 2026, with set capital requirements and no automatic free pass for firms already operating.
items.whatItMeans: A virtual asset is a digital asset like a cryptocurrency or token. A custodian is a firm that safely holds assets for clients, so crypto custody rules are mostly about protecting the secret keys that control the coins. Licensing means a firm must get official permission and meet set standards before it can operate. By covering dealers, custodians, advisers, and managers, Hong Kong is trying to regulate the whole chain of crypto services, not just the exchanges.
clients: More crypto firms will need a licence. Dealers, custodians, and advisers that were lightly regulated must now prepare to apply and meet capital and conduct rules, so they need legal help early.
There is no easy grandfathering. Existing operators do not get an automatic pass, so even established firms must bring themselves up to standard or risk being shut out.
Traditional finance players can enter with confidence. Clear rules make banks and asset managers more comfortable offering crypto services, and they will want advice on how to do it compliantly.
lawFirms: It creates a pipeline of licensing work. Each new regime means applications, compliance systems, and ongoing advice, which is steady fee income for financial regulatory teams.
It rewards early specialists. Firms that build virtual-asset expertise now become the natural choice as the rules land, winning the best clients in a growing market.
It blends skills juniors can grow into. The work sits across financial regulation, technology, and funds, giving trainees a chance to build a modern, in-demand niche.

sources.title: Sidley Austin: Hong Kong to enhance the virtual asset licensing regime to cover advisers and managers

items.headline: HSBC Buys Out Hang Seng Bank in a US$14 Billion Take-Private
items.whatsTheNews: HSBC is taking Hang Seng Bank, one of Hong Kong's best-known lenders, fully private in a deal worth about US$14 billion. HSBC already owned most of Hang Seng and offered HK$155 per share for the rest, around 30% above the market price before the news. Independent shareholders backed the plan, with about 85.75% voting in favour, well past the 75% needed. Hang Seng shares stopped trading in mid-January and were delisted from the Hong Kong stock exchange on 27 January 2026.
items.whatItMeans: Taking a company private means buying up all its publicly traded shares so it is no longer listed on the stock exchange. Because HSBC already controlled Hang Seng, this is called privatising a subsidiary. The premium, the 30% extra over the market price, is what HSBC paid to persuade outside shareholders to sell. Once delisted, the public can no longer buy or sell the shares, and the company answers to one owner instead of the market.
clients: A deal this size needs careful steering. Strict takeover rules and a shareholder vote apply, so HSBC and Hang Seng each need lawyers to run the process correctly.
Minority shareholders need protection. The rules exist so outside investors get a fair price and proper information, and advisers make sure the offer and disclosures meet those standards.
Other companies take note. A large, successful privatisation can prompt other controlling shareholders to consider buying out their own listed units, creating more potential deals.
lawFirms: Privatisations are premium M&A work. They are complex, high-value, and heavily regulated, so they generate large fees and long instructions for corporate teams.
Several teams get involved at once. Corporate, banking, and regulatory lawyers all play a part, which spreads work across the firm.

A busy take-private market means more deals to staff. When controlling shareholders move to privatise undervalued listed companies, firms with strong M&A benches win the mandates and bring in juniors to help.

sources.title: SCMP: Hang Seng Bank shareholders approve HSBC's US$14 billion privatisation bid

items.headline: Hong Kong's First Big Cybersecurity Law Lands on Critical Companies
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's Protection of Critical Infrastructures (Computer Systems) Ordinance came into force on 1 January 2026. It is the city's first law squarely aimed at protecting the computer systems behind essential services. It covers operators in eight sectors, including banking and finance, energy, telecoms, healthcare, and transport. Those operators must set up security teams, run regular audits, and report serious cyber incidents to a new government commissioner within a tight deadline, with penalties for getting it wrong running up to HK$5 million.
items.whatItMeans: Critical infrastructure means the systems a city cannot function without, like the networks that run banks, hospitals, and power. A critical infrastructure operator is a company the government designates as running one of these essential systems. The law sets three duties: keep your own house in order (a local office and a security team), prevent attacks (security plans and audits), and report quickly when something goes wrong. The goal is to stop a single cyber attack from knocking out services that millions of people rely on.
clients: Designated companies have real work to do. They must build security teams, write formal plans, and set up fast incident reporting, so they need legal and compliance advice to meet the new duties.

The duties flow down to suppliers. A bank that must report a breach within hours cannot wait a day for its cloud provider, so it will rewrite contracts to demand quick notice and audit rights from every vendor.
The cost of failure is high. Fines reach HK$5 million with daily penalties for ongoing breaches, so clients will pay for advice that keeps them on the right side of the rules.
lawFirms: A fresh stream of advisory work just opened. Firms help clients work out whether they are caught, draft security policies, and handle incident reporting, which is steady, recurring work.
It drives a wave of contract redrafting. Because operators must push duties onto suppliers, firms get hired to renegotiate vendor and cloud agreements across whole supply chains.

It rewards firms with tech and regulatory depth. Lawyers who understand both data and financial regulation are best placed to win this work, and it is a fast-growing field for juniors to build a niche.

sources.title: Mayer Brown: Hong Kong issues the Code of Practice under the Critical Infrastructures Ordinance
Communications Authority: Protection of Critical Infrastructures (Computer Systems) Ordinance

items.headline: Hong Kong's Biggest Listed Companies Must Now Report Their Climate Impact
items.whatsTheNews: From the start of 2026, Hong Kong's largest listed companies must report detailed climate information under new stock exchange rules. The rules follow global standards known as the ISSB, and they cover how a company manages climate risks and how much greenhouse gas it produces. The biggest companies, those in the Hang Seng Composite LargeCap Index, must comply on a mandatory basis for financial years starting on or after 1 January 2026. Smaller listed companies report on a softer "comply or explain" basis for now, with tougher parts phased in over time.
items.whatItMeans: Climate disclosure means telling investors, in the annual report, how climate change affects the business and how much pollution it causes. The ISSB standards are a common global rulebook for this, so investors can compare companies across countries. "Scope 1 and 2 emissions" are the greenhouse gases a company produces directly and through the energy it buys. Comply or explain means a company either follows the rule or publicly explains why it has not, a gentle way to ease companies in before a rule becomes compulsory.
clients: Big listed companies have real reporting to do. They must gather climate and emissions data and present it properly, so they need advisers to help them meet the standard and avoid getting it wrong.
Boards face new responsibility. Directors are accountable for what the company discloses, so they want legal advice on how to report honestly and protect themselves.
It affects fundraising and reputation. Investors increasingly look at climate data, so getting disclosure right helps a company raise money and avoid criticism.
lawFirms: A steady advisory line opens up. Firms help clients understand the rules, set up reporting, and manage the legal risk in what they publish, which recurs each reporting season.
It blends corporate and ESG skills. Lawyers who understand both listing rules and sustainability are well placed, and ESG is one of the fastest-growing areas in the profession.
It feeds governance and disputes work later. As disclosure becomes mandatory, mistakes can lead to regulator questions or claims, giving advisory and litigation teams more to do.

sources.title: Norton Rose Fulbright: new ISSB-aligned climate disclosure regime under the HKEX Listing Rules
